content:
# Joshua Chapter 24 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 248-249 | **Source Image:** `../../source-texts/hebrew-OT/JOS_024_page_001.jpg`

1. And Yehoshua gathered all the tribes of Yisrael to Shechem, and he called for the elders of Yisrael and for their heads and for their judges and for their officers, and they stationed themselves before God.

2. And Yehoshua said to all the people, "Thus says YHWH, God of Yisrael: 'On the other side of the River your fathers dwelt from of old — Terach, the father of Avraham and the father of Nachor — and they served other gods.

3. And I took your father, Avraham, from the other side of the River, and I caused him to walk through all the land of Kena'an, and I multiplied his seed, and I gave to him Yitschak.

4. And I gave to Yitschak, Yaakov and Esav. And I gave to Esav Mount Se'ir to possess it, and Yaakov and his sons went down [to] Mitsrayim.

5. And I sent Moshe and Aharon, and I struck Mitsrayim according to that which I did in its midst, and afterward I brought you out.

6. And I brought your fathers out from Mitsrayim, and you came to the sea. And Mitsrayim pursued after your fathers with chariots and with horsemen to the Sea of Reeds.

7. And they cried out to YHWH, and he set darkness between you and between Mitsrayim, and he brought the sea upon them and covered them. And your eyes saw that which I did in Mitsrayim. And you dwelt in the wilderness many days.

8. And I brought you to the land of the Amorites who were dwelling on the other side of the Yarden, and they fought with you. And I gave them into your hand, and you possessed their land, and I destroyed them from before you.

9. And Balak son of Tsippor, king of Moav, arose and fought against Yisrael. And he sent and called for Bil'am son of Be'or to curse you.

10. And I was not willing to listen to Bil'am, and he surely blessed you, and I delivered you from his hand.

11. And you crossed over the Yarden and came to Yericho. And the lords of Yericho fought against you — the Amorites and the Perizzites and the Kena'anites and the Chittites and the Girgashites, the Chivvites and the Yevusites — and I gave them into your hand.

12. And I sent the hornet before you, and it drove them out from before you — two kings of the Amorites — not by your sword and not by your bow.

13. And I gave to you a land for which you did not toil, and cities which you did not build, and you dwell in them; vineyards and olive groves which you did not plant, you [are] eating.'

14. And now, fear YHWH and serve him in completeness and in truth. And put away the gods which your fathers served on the other side of the River and in Mitsrayim, and serve YHWH.

15. And if [it is] evil in your eyes to serve YHWH, choose for yourselves today whom you will serve — whether the gods which your fathers served that [were] on the other side of the River, or the gods of the Amorites in whose land you [are] dwelling. And as for me and my house, we will serve YHWH."

16. And the people answered and said, "Far be it from us to forsake YHWH, to serve other gods.

17. For YHWH our God, he [is] the one who brought us and our fathers up from the land of Mitsrayim, from the house of slaves, and who did before our eyes these great signs, and he kept us in all the way which we walked and among all the peoples through whose midst we passed.

18. And YHWH drove out all the peoples, and the Amorites dwelling in the land, from before us. Also we will serve YHWH, for he [is] our God."

19. And Yehoshua said to the people, "You are not able to serve YHWH, for he [is] a holy God; he [is] a jealous God. He will not forgive your transgressions and your sins.

20. If you forsake YHWH and serve foreign gods, then he will turn and do evil to you and consume you, after that he has done good to you."

21. And the people said to Yehoshua, "No, for YHWH we will serve."

22. And Yehoshua said to the people, "Witnesses [are] you against yourselves that you have chosen for yourselves YHWH, to serve him." And they said, "Witnesses."

23. "And now, put away the foreign gods which [are] in your midst, and incline your heart to YHWH, God of Yisrael."

24. And the people said to Yehoshua, "YHWH our God we will serve, and his voice we will obey."

25. And Yehoshua cut a covenant for the people on that day, and he set for them a statute and a judgment in Shechem.

26. And Yehoshua wrote these words in the scroll of the Torah of God. And he took a great stone and raised it up there, under the oak which [was] in the sanctuary of YHWH.

27. And Yehoshua said to all the people, "Behold, this stone shall be for a witness against us, for it has heard all the sayings of YHWH which he spoke with us. And it shall be for a witness against you, lest you deny your God."

28. And Yehoshua sent the people away, each to his inheritance.

29. And it was after these things, and Yehoshua son of Nun, servant of YHWH, died, [being] a son of one hundred and ten years.

30. And they buried him in the border of his inheritance, in Timnat-Serach, which [is] in the hill country of Efrayim, on the north of Mount Ga'ash.

31. And Yisrael served YHWH all the days of Yehoshua and all the days of the elders who prolonged days after Yehoshua, and who knew all the work of YHWH which he had done for Yisrael.

32. And the bones of Yosef, which the sons of Yisrael brought up from Mitsrayim, they buried in Shechem, in the portion of the field which Yaakov acquired from the sons of Chamor, the father of Shechem, for one hundred qesitah; and they became [an inheritance] for the sons of Yosef.

33. And El'azar son of Aharon died, and they buried him in Giv'at Pinechas his son, which was given to him in the hill country of Efrayim.

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

**Manuscript and Source:** Translated from Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 248-249, as photographed by the West Semitic Research Project (Bruce E. Zuckerman). Joshua 24 is the final chapter of the book, recording the covenant ceremony at Shechem, Joshua's farewell, death, and burial — along with the burial notices for Joseph's bones and Eleazar the priest.

**Verse 1 — וַיֶּאֱסֹף (And he gathered):** The Hiphil wayyiqtol of אסף indicates that Yehoshua actively convened this assembly. The location שְׁכֶמָה (to Shechem) uses the directional he suffix. Shechem is chosen for its covenantal associations — the place where Avraham first received YHWH's promise in the land (Gen 12:6) and where Yaakov buried the foreign gods (Gen 35:4). The verb וַיִּתְיַצְּבוּ (and they stationed themselves) is the Hithpael of יצב, implying a formal, deliberate positioning — a covenantal stance before God (לִפְנֵי הָאֱלֹהִים).

**Verse 2 — בְּעֵבֶר הַנָּהָר (on the other side of the River):** This phrase recurs in vv. 2, 3, 14, 15 as a key motif. "The River" (הַנָּהָר) without further specification refers to the Euphrates in standard biblical usage. The phrase frames the entire speech: Yisrael's origins were in Mesopotamian idolatry, YHWH called them out, and now they must choose. The mention of Terach (תֶּרַח) serving other gods is significant — the idolatrous past extends to Avraham's own father.

**Verse 2 — וַיַּעַבְדוּ אֱלֹהִים אֲחֵרִים:** "And they served other gods." The verb עבד (to serve) becomes the chapter's defining keyword, appearing repeatedly in both its idolatrous sense (serving other gods) and its proper sense (serving YHWH). This deliberate repetition structures the entire covenant dialogue.

**Verse 10 — בָרוֹךְ בֵּרַךְ (surely blessed):** The infinitive absolute construction (בָּרֵךְ בֵּרַךְ) is preserved as "he surely blessed" to capture the emphatic Hebrew doubling. This refers to the Balaam narrative of Numbers 22-24, where Balak hired Bil'am to curse Yisrael but YHWH turned the curse into blessing.

**Verse 12 — הַצִּרְעָה (the hornet):** The Hebrew צִרְעָה is rendered literally as "hornet." Scholars debate whether this is literal (stinging insects), metaphorical (for panic/terror), or a reference to Egyptian military campaigns (the bee being a symbol of Lower Egypt). The Leningrad Codex reading is unambiguous — the definite noun is singular. The LXX reads τὴν σφηκίαν (the wasp/hornet), supporting the literal reading.

**Verse 19 — אֱלֹהִים קְדֹשִׁים (a holy God):** The plural form קְדֹשִׁים with the singular meaning is a plural of majesty (pluralis majestatis), like אֱלֹהִים itself. Yehoshua's warning that the people "are not able" to serve YHWH is striking — it functions as a provocation to deepen their commitment, not as a prohibition. The phrase אֵל־קַנּוֹא (a jealous God) echoes Exodus 20:5 and Deuteronomy 5:9.

**Verse 26 — הָאַלָּה (the oak):** The Leningrad Codex reads הָאַלָּה (the oak/terebinth), the same tree-type associated with Shechem in Genesis 35:4, where Yaakov buried the foreign gods and earrings. The covenantal geography is precise and deliberate: the stone witness is placed under the same sacred tree, in the sanctuary (מִקְדַּשׁ) of YHWH. This links the covenant renewal directly to the patriarchal narrative.

**Verse 27 — כִּי־הִיא שָׁמְעָה (for it has heard):** The stone is personified as a witness that "heard" (שָׁמְעָה, feminine singular, agreeing with אֶבֶן) all the words YHWH spoke. This is a characteristic ancient Near Eastern covenant motif — natural elements serve as enduring witnesses to treaty agreements (cf. Deuteronomy 32:1, where heaven and earth are summoned as witnesses).

**Verse 29 — בֶּן־מֵאָה וָעֶשֶׂר שָׁנִים (a son of one hundred and ten years):** One hundred and ten years was the ideal Egyptian lifespan. Yosef also died at one hundred and ten (Gen 50:26). This forms an inclusio: Yosef's bones are brought up from Mitsrayim (v. 32), and Yehoshua, who completes the work Yosef's story anticipated, dies at the same age.

**Verse 32 — קְשִׂיטָה (qesitah):** An archaic unit of currency (בְּמֵאָה קְשִׂיטָה, "for one hundred qesitah"). The term appears only in Genesis 33:19 and Job 42:11 elsewhere. Its exact value is unknown. The LXX renders it ἑκατὸν ἀμνῶν ("one hundred lambs"), suggesting an ancient interpretive tradition linking the term to livestock. The Leningrad Codex reading is retained untranslated as a transliteration.

**Verse 33 — Final verse of Joshua:** The book concludes with three burial notices: Yehoshua (v. 30), Yosef's bones (v. 32), and El'azar (v. 33). This triple ending ties together the Mosaic generation (El'azar), the patriarchal promise (Yosef's bones in the land), and the conquest generation (Yehoshua). Giv'at Pinechas ("the hill of Pinechas") is El'azar's son's territory in the hill country of Efrayim.
